studies.title: Living with diabetes in a multi-ethnic community
studies.setting: UK primary care, multi-ethnic London borough
studies.participants: 25 adults with T2DM (12 South Asian, 13 White British)
key_findings: Diabetes experienced as an unwelcome identity shift
Dietary management creates social tension at family meals

quotes.text: It changed who I am. I'm not the same person anymore.
My mother-in-law cooks everything with ghee. I can't refuse without causing offence.

studies.title: Perceptions and experiences of taking oral hypoglycaemics
studies.participants: 40 patients with T2DM starting oral medication
key_findings: Medication seen as proof of personal failure to control diet
Tablet-taking normalises the condition over time
Side effects rarely discussed with clinicians

quotes.text: Taking tablets means I've failed, doesn't it? I couldn't do it with food alone.
After a while you just pop the pill and forget about it. It becomes normal.

studies.title: Making sense of diabetes self-management
studies.participants: 12 adults with T2DM attending education programme
key_findings: Knowledge empowers but also creates anxiety about complications
Peer support from other patients is more valued than clinical advice

quotes.text: Knowing what could happen scares me but also makes me try harder.
The others in the group understand. My doctor doesn't live with it.

studies.title: Conducting systematic reviews of qualitative studies of chronic illness
key_findings: Social identity as 'diabetic' resisted by most participants
Technology (glucose monitors) both liberates and constrains

quotes.text: I'm tired of thinking about it every single day. It never stops.
I don't tell people. It's my business, not theirs.

studies.title: Diagnosis and self-management of type 2 diabetes
studies.participants: 40 newly diagnosed T2DM patients, longitudinal (over 1 year)
key_findings: Initial relief at diagnosis ('at least it's not cancer') shifts to chronic burden
Dietary advice perceived as contradictory and confusing
Partners become informal health monitors creating relationship tension

quotes.text: At first I thought, well at least it's manageable. Now I'm not so sure.
My wife watches everything I eat now. It causes rows.

themes.label: Identity disruption
themes.description: Diabetes challenges existing self-concept and social identity

themes.label: Daily burden of management
themes.description: Constant cognitive and practical demands of self-management

themes.label: Social navigation
themes.description: Managing diabetes within family, cultural, and social contexts

themes.label: Empowerment through knowledge
themes.description: Knowledge and self-monitoring as sources of control and anxiety

themes.label: Living with a contested self
themes.description: Overarching theme: diabetes creates ongoing tension between the pre-diagnosis self and the 'diabetic' identity, mediated by social context and knowledge

cerqual_findings.finding_text: People with T2DM experience diagnosis as a threat to their existing identity, resisting the label of 'diabetic' while gradually incorporating management into daily routines.
Self-management creates ongoing daily burden that participants describe as exhausting, with 'diabetes fatigue' emerging in long-term management.
Dietary management is the most socially disruptive aspect, creating tension with family members and within cultural food practices.
Peer support from others with diabetes is valued above professional clinical advice for day-to-day management strategies.
